introduction: Percentage means 'per hundred'. It is one of the most important and highest-weightage topics in SSC CGL Quantitative Aptitude, forming the base for Profit-Loss, Discount, SI-CI, Data Interpretation and Mixture problems. Mastering percentage-to-fraction conversion and ratio method makes calculations 3-4x faster.

concepts.explanation: x% means x out of 100, i.e. x/100. To convert percentage to fraction, divide by 100. To convert fraction to percentage, multiply by 100.
% = (A/B) x 100. Always identify which is the base (denominator).
% change = (Change/Original value) x 100. 'Original' is always the base for percentage increase/decrease questions.
Any percentage relation can be converted into a ratio using fraction equivalents. Example: A is 20% more than B => A:B = 6:5 (since 20% = 1/5, so A = B + B/5 = 6B/5).
If a quantity changes by x% then y%, net change = x + y + xy/100 (use + for increase, - for decrease of that term).
When something increases by x%, the new value becomes base for next % change — never apply next % on the original value.

formulas: A is x% of B => A = (x/100) x B
A is x% more than B => A = B x (100+x)/100
A is x% less than B => A = B x (100-x)/100
If A is x% more than B, then B is [x/(100+x)] x 100 % less than A
If A is x% less than B, then B is [x/(100-x)] x 100 % more than A
Successive % change (both increase): Net% = x + y + xy/100
Successive % change (both decrease): Net% = -(x + y - xy/100)
Successive % change (one inc, one dec): Net% = x - y - xy/100
Price-Consumption-Expenditure: Expenditure = Price x Consumption; if price changes x%, consumption must change by [-x/(100+x)]x100% to keep expenditure same
Error % = (Difference between correct & wrong value / Correct value) x 100

question_types.type_name: Basic Percentage Calculation (of a number / A as % of B)
question_types.identification_method: Direct language like 'x% of y' or 'what % is A of B'.
question_types.when_to_use: Use fraction conversion (e.g. 12.5% = 1/8) instead of direct multiplication for speed.
step_by_step: Convert % to fraction using standard fraction table
Multiply fraction with the given number
Simplify to get answer
question_types.shortcut: Memorize fraction table 1/2 to 1/25; for odd % like 37.5%, break into 25%+12.5% or use 3/8.
common_mistakes: Multiplying by % value directly instead of dividing by 100 first
Confusing 'of' with wrong base number

practice_questions.q: Find 25% of 640.
Find 37.5% of 800.
What is 16.66% of 900?
Evaluate 25% of 50% of 500.
45 is what % of 180?
What % of 250 is 90?

question_types.identification_method: Phrases like 'A is how much % more/less than B'.
question_types.when_to_use: Use ratio method: convert given data into ratio A:B, then find difference/base x 100.
step_by_step: Write A:B in simplest ratio (from values or fractions given)
Find difference between A and B in the ratio
Divide difference by the base term (B if 'more/less than B') and multiply by 100
question_types.shortcut: % more than B = [(A-B)/B]x100; % less than A = [(A-B)/A]x100 — base changes with wording, be careful.
common_mistakes: Using wrong base (dividing by A instead of B or vice versa)
Not converting fractions to common ratio before comparing

practice_questions.q: A=100, B=150. B is how much % more than A?
A=2/3, B=3/2. B is how much % more than A?
A quantity changed from 4/5 to 5/6. Find % change.
If A is 34% less than B, find A:B.
If A is 28% more than B, find A:B.

question_types.identification_method: Two or more % changes applied one after another on the same quantity (increase then increase/decrease).
question_types.when_to_use: Use net% formula: x+y+xy/100 for two increases, or chain multiplication of fractions for 3+ changes.
step_by_step: List all % changes with correct sign (+ for increase, - for decrease)
For 2 changes, apply net% = x+y+xy/100 directly
For 3+ changes or when final value given, multiply base by respective fractions e.g. 105/100 x 95/100
Solve for unknown (final or initial value)
question_types.shortcut: Order of successive % changes does not matter — net effect is the same regardless of sequence.
common_mistakes: Adding percentages directly without the xy/100 correction term
Applying second % change on original value instead of new value

practice_questions.q: Population increases 5% then decreases 5%. If final population is 47,880, find initial.
A number increased by 16%, then 20%, then decreased by 40%. Find net % decrease.
Price rises 20% then 25%. What single % decrease restores original price?

question_types.when_to_use: Use relation Expenditure = Price x Consumption. Represent both price and consumption as ratios, multiply for expenditure ratio.
step_by_step: Convert price change to ratio (old:new)
Convert consumption change to ratio (old:new) — this may be unknown (find using given expenditure change)
Expenditure ratio = Price ratio x Consumption ratio
Solve unknown ratio term, convert to %
question_types.shortcut: If expenditure must stay constant, required % change in consumption = [-x/(100+x)] x 100 when price increases by x%.
common_mistakes: Forgetting expenditure = price x consumption, not price + consumption
Sign errors when price increases but expenditure decreases

practice_questions.q: Price of rice up 25%, consumption down 30%. Find % change in expenditure.
Price of movie ticket up 12%, sale up 8%. Find % change in income.
Price of sugar rises 30%, expenditure rises 12%. Find % decrease needed in consumption.
practice_questions.solution: 10:13 price ratio vs 100:112 expenditure; consumption ratio 130:112, decrease=18/130=13.85%

question_types.identification_method: Mentions of 'voters', 'registered voters', 'valid/invalid votes', 'candidates', 'won by margin'.
question_types.when_to_use: Use Tree Method when % is given on TOTAL votes; use Successive (chain) method when % is given only on VALID votes.
step_by_step: Draw tree: Total voters -> voted/not voted -> valid/invalid -> candidate A/candidate B
Assign % at each branch based on question wording
Write margin/difference equation using variables
Solve for total voters using given margin value
question_types.shortcut: Candidate share given on 'valid votes' => successive multiplication (chain); on 'total/registered voters' => tree with subtraction.
common_mistakes: Mixing tree and successive methods
Forgetting to subtract invalid/NOTA votes before finding candidate share
Misreading base (total voters vs valid votes)

practice_questions.q: 5% didn't vote, 10% invalid, winner got 60% of valid votes, won by 17,271. Find registered voters.
12% didn't vote, 84 blank votes, winner got 54% of all voters, won by 1456. Find total voters.
35% voted P, 50% of remaining voted Q, rest didn't vote. Diff between P-voters and non-voters=1000. Find total eligible voters.

question_types.identification_method: Mentions salary/income split into expenditure categories and savings, or 'income=expenditure+savings'.
question_types.when_to_use: Use Income = Expenditure + Savings identity; represent all parts as % of income=100 and solve missing part.
step_by_step: Set total income = 100%
Subtract all given expenditure %s to find savings % (or vice versa)
If income changes, apply new % to find new expenditure/savings keeping the identity balanced
Convert final % difference into required answer
question_types.shortcut: For 'savings constant, income increases x%' type: % increase in expenditure = (x% of income)/(original expenditure) x100.
common_mistakes: Forgetting income=expenditure+savings identity
Not recalculating base % when income changes

practice_questions.q: Expenditure=45% of income. Income rises 15%, savings unchanged. Find % rise in expenditure.
practice_questions.solution: 100=45+55; new income=115, savings=55 unchanged, new exp=60; rise=15/45x100=33.33%

practice_questions.q: Income=80000; spends 15% education,40% household,10% GPF,15% savings, rest EMI. Find EMI.

question_types.identification_method: Two subjects/tests mentioned with pass/fail % and 'both' condition.
question_types.when_to_use: Use Venn diagram / set theory: Failed(A or B) = Failed(A)+Failed(B)-Failed(both).
step_by_step: Convert pass% to fail% for each subject if needed
Apply Fail(A∪B) = Fail(A)+Fail(B)-Fail(A∩B)
Find Pass(both) = 100% - Fail(A∪B)
Use given actual numbers to scale % to real total
question_types.shortcut: Draw simple 2-circle Venn and fill only 3 known regions; 4th (pass both) = 100 - sum of other three.
common_mistakes: Adding fail percentages without subtracting overlap
Confusing 'failed in both' with 'failed in at least one'

practice_questions.q: 45% passed History, 60% passed Hindi, 8% failed both. Find % passed both.
practice_questions.solution: Fail History=55%,Fail Hindi=40%; Fail(A∪B)=55+40-8=87? recompute via diagram: pass both=13%

practice_questions.q: 54% failed GA, 45% failed QT, 16% failed both, 136 passed both. Find total candidates.

question_types.identification_method: Student/person performs wrong operation (multiply instead of divide, wrong fraction) — 'error %' or 'percentage difference' asked.
question_types.when_to_use: Convert correct and wrong operation results into a ratio, then find % difference over correct value.
step_by_step: Write ratio of Wrong result : Correct result
Find difference between the two
Error% = (difference/Correct value) x 100
question_types.shortcut: For 'multiplied by a/b instead of b/a', ratio = a²:b², error% = (a²-b²)/b² x100 (use correct as base — read question carefully).
common_mistakes: Using wrong value as base instead of correct value
Sign confusion — is result too high or too low

practice_questions.q: Multiplied by 4/3 instead of 3/4. Find error %.
Divided by 8 instead of multiplying by 8. Find % difference.

question_types.identification_method: Mentions water % in a fruit/substance changing due to drying, 'pulp content' problems.
question_types.when_to_use: Pulp (non-water part) remains CONSTANT; only water % changes — always equate pulp quantities.
step_by_step: Find pulp % initially = 100 - water%
Pulp quantity = Total weight x pulp%
Since pulp is constant, set up equation: Old pulp = New Total weight x New pulp%
Solve for new total weight; reduction = old total - new total
question_types.shortcut: Directly equate: Old Weight x Old Pulp% = New Weight x New Pulp%.
common_mistakes: Focusing on water content instead of constant pulp content
Forgetting to convert % to same base

practice_questions.q: 15kg grapes, 90% water. After drying, water=85%. Find reduced weight.

identification_tricks: If question gives direct 'x% of y' — Basic % type, use fraction shortcut.
If question compares two quantities with 'more/less than' — use ratio method, watch the base.
If % applied one after another on same quantity — Successive % change, use x+y+xy/100.
If price, consumption/sale, and expenditure/income all mentioned — Price-Consumption-Expenditure type.
If voters/candidates/election mentioned — check whether % is on 'total' or 'valid' votes to pick Tree vs Successive method.
If income split into parts + savings — use Income=Expenditure+Savings identity.
If two subjects/exams with 'failed both/either' — Venn diagram approach.
If wrong operation mentioned (multiplied/divided incorrectly) — Error % type.
If fruit/vegetable with water content drying up — Wet-Dry (Pulp constant) type.
solving_approach: Step 1: Read the question fully and underline what is asked (final value, % change, base value etc).
Step 2: Identify the TYPE from the identification tricks list above.
Step 3: Convert all percentages into simplest fractions/ratios wherever possible.
Step 4: Set up ratio chains or equations based on the type-specific approach.
Step 5: Solve using cross-multiplication or direct fraction shortcuts — avoid decimal calculation unless necessary.
Step 6: Sanity check the answer against options (elimination) before finalizing.
shortcuts: Memorize fraction-percentage table up to 1/25 — saves 15-20 seconds per question.
A:B ratio method is faster than % formula for 'more/less than' comparison questions.
For successive % changes, order does not matter — arrange in most convenient calculation order.
For election Tree method problems, always draw the tree — mental math leads to errors.
25% of 800 = 800% of 25 (cross-multiplication property) — useful for reverse calculation questions.
For Venn diagram overlap type, use 100 - (sum of exclusive fail%) formula directly.
common_mistakes: Not identifying the correct BASE for percentage change (always original/previous value, not final).
Adding percentages directly in successive change without the xy/100 correction term.
Mixing Tree method and Successive method in election questions.
Forgetting pulp/non-water content remains constant in wet-dry fruit problems.
Using wrong value (wrong vs correct) as denominator in error % questions.
Rounding off too early leading to wrong final decimal answers.

practice_questions_master.q: If A is 12.5% more than B, find A:B.
A number is increased by 10% and then decreased by 10%. Net effect?
In an election, 5% did not vote, 10% votes invalid, winner got 60% of valid votes and won by 17271 votes. Find total registered voters.
45% passed subject A, 60% passed subject B, 8% failed both. Find % passed both subjects.

detailed_solutions_note: Detailed step-by-step solutions are embedded within each question_type's practice_questions array above (see 'solution' field) for quick reference.
pyq_patterns: SSC CGL frequently asks Successive % change combined with Election problems as a 2-in-1 tricky question.
Price-Consumption-Expenditure questions appear almost every year, often disguised as 'household expenditure' or 'shop revenue' problems.
Fraction-based comparison (A is x% more/less than B) is a near-guaranteed 1 question per exam shift.
Wet-Dry fruit (constant pulp) problems appear in Tier-1 and Tier-2 with different fruit names but same logic.
Venn diagram pass-fail problems are common in CGL Tier-2 and CHSL as a slightly harder variant.
quick_revision: x% more => multiply by (100+x)/100; x% less => multiply by (100-x)/100
Election: Tree method for %-of-total, Successive method for %-of-valid
Pulp constant in wet-dry fruit problems
exam_tips: Always convert percentages to fractions before multiplying — big speed boost.
In multi-step problems, write down the ratio chain on paper instead of doing pure mental math.
For election questions, spend 5 extra seconds identifying total vs valid votes base — this avoids the most common error.
Practice the standard fraction table daily until it becomes instant recall.
Time target: attempt basic % questions in under 20 seconds, complex multi-concept ones in 45-60 seconds.
